diagnosisAssessment:
CORRECT parts: (1) "in discuss/explore mode" (discuss-phase.md line 29) is an invented qualifier — brainstorming/SKILL.md defines no modes anywhere; line 149 explicitly states the Visual Companion is "Available as a tool — not a mode." So the user is right that the phrase has no basis in the skill. (2) The user is right that the phrase creates risk: per the writing-skills CSO failure mode, a wrapper that adds a workflow-summarizing qualifier ("in discuss/explore mode") around a skill makes the model follow the wrapper's framing instead of the skill body. "Explore mode" semantically suggests a lightweight, talk-only pass, giving implicit license to stop after the discussion bullets in discuss-phase.md step 2 (Scope/Concerns/Dependencies/Risks/Alternatives) and never reach brainstorming checklist items 6-8 (write SPEC.md, self-review, user review).

WRONG / INCOMPLETE parts: The diagnosis stops at the mode phrase, but the file evidence shows the structural cause is DEEPER and partly independent of the phrase. discuss-phase.md is itself a competing, paraphrased re-summary of brainstorming that does NOT mention SPEC.md at all. Its own <process> (lines 19-49) lists four steps — Load Phase Context, Brainstorm, Document Decisions (DECISIONS.md), Update State — and its terminal artifact is DECISIONS.md, not SPEC.md. Its <offer_next> block (lines 51-61) announces "DISCUSSION COMPLETE / {X} decisions documented" with zero reference to a spec. So even with the mode phrase deleted, the workflow's own checklist and completion banner define "done" as "decisions documented," which actively competes with brainstorming's step-6 SPEC.md write. The model can satisfy the wrapper's visible 4-step process and emit the completion banner without ever producing SPEC.md. The phrase is one symptom; the wrapper's divergent process+banner is the load-bearing defect.
whatsMissing:
Three things the user is not seeing:

1. A PATH-CONTRACT MISMATCH that no proposed fix addresses. brainstorming step 6 (SKILL.md line 29 and line 111) writes the spec to `.quantis/phases/{N}.{M}-{slug}/SPEC.md`. But the downstream consumer plan.md (Planning Lock, step 1) checks `grep -q "FINALIZED" ".quantis/SPEC.md"` — the ROOT milestone spec, a DIFFERENT file. So even when brainstorming DOES produce a phase SPEC.md correctly, plan.md is validating the wrong path and (a) won't catch a missing phase SPEC.md, and (b) the phase SPEC.md is never required to contain "FINALIZED". Any post-brainstorming gate the user adds to discuss-phase.md must assert the phase-level path, and plan.md's lock must be reconciled to the same path, or the gate and the consumer will check different files.

2. The user's gate, if naively written as "SPEC.md must exist," collides with brainstorming step 9. SKILL.md checklist item 9 (line 32) and lines 62/66/133-136 make invoking writing-plans the AUTO terminal state. A discuss-phase gate that runs "after brainstorming completes" runs after writing-plans has already been auto-invoked. The gate must be scoped to "brainstorming steps 1-8 only" (as the CLAUDE.md ground truth already notes) AND discuss-phase must decide whether it even wants step 9 to fire here — because /discuss-phase's own <offer_next> (line 58) tells the user to run /plan next, meaning Quantis intends discuss and plan to be SEPARATE commands. brainstorming's built-in auto-transition to writing-plans directly fights that separation. This is an unresolved design conflict the mode-phrase fix doesn't touch.

3. discuss-phase.md never tells the model to create one TodoWrite task per brainstorming checklist item. The skill's enforcement engine (SKILL.md line 22: "You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order") plus the using-quantis bootstrap's Commitment mechanism (TodoWrite-per-item) is what actually makes step 6 fire. If the wrapper re-summarizes the process into its own 4 steps without surfacing the skill's 9-item checklist as todos, the skill's own enforcement never engages.
recommendedFix:
Single best fix, in priority order, all in discuss-phase.md (with one reconciling edit to plan.md):

A. DELETE the invented qualifier. Change line 29 from "Read and follow ...SKILL.md in discuss/explore mode." to a pure trigger+announce: "**Read and follow `.agents/skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md`.** Announce: 'Using brainstorming to design phase {N}.{M}.' Create one TodoWrite task per checklist item (steps 1-8)." Do not paraphrase the skill's process.

B. STOP the wrapper from competing with the skill. The discuss-phase <process> should TRIGGER the skill, not re-list a divergent 4-step process whose terminal artifact is DECISIONS.md. Keep the Quantis-specific value-add (Load Phase Context from ROADMAP; record decisions in DECISIONS.md) but subordinate it to the skill and make SPEC.md the named deliverable.

C. ADD a top-anchored AND end-restated HARD-GATE naming the exact artifact and path, scoped to steps 1-8 (to avoid the step-9 auto-invoke collision). Bookend per the lost-in-the-middle evidence — do not bury it at the tail. Concretely, near the top of <process>: "<HARD-GATE> /discuss-phase is NOT complete until `.quantis/phases/{N}.{M}-{slug}/SPEC.md` exists and the user has approved it (brainstorming steps 1-8). Do NOT emit the completion banner before this file exists. </HARD-GATE>" Then make the <offer_next> banner a post-condition CHECK, not a claim: replace "{X} decisions documented" with an explicit verified line, e.g. a bash check `test -f .quantis/phases/${N}-${SLUG}/SPEC.md && echo "SPEC.md ✓" || echo "HALT: SPEC.md missing"`. A verifiable file-check post-condition is far more robust than prose (per the Reflexion/Azure "produce a verifiable artifact" evidence).

D. RESOLVE step-9 explicitly. Add one line: "/discuss-phase stops at brainstorming step 8. Do NOT auto-invoke writing-plans here; planning is a separate command (/plan). If brainstorming's step 9 fires, halt and hand back to the user with the /plan offer." This makes the discuss/plan separation that <offer_next> already implies actually enforceable.

E. RECONCILE the consumer. Fix plan.md Planning Lock (step 1) to validate the SAME phase-level path the gate asserts: `test -f "$PHASE_DIR/SPEC.md" || echo "Error: phase SPEC.md missing — run /discuss-phase first"`. Otherwise the producer gate and the consumer check different files and the contract stays broken.
alternativesConsidered:
Alt 1 — User's minimal fix only (delete phrase + add "SPEC.md exists" gate): Better than nothing and removes the clearest symptom, but treats the symptom. It leaves the competing 4-step wrapper process and DECISIONS.md-centric completion banner intact, leaves the phase-vs-root path mismatch with plan.md, and an unscoped "SPEC.md exists" gate collides with the step-9 auto-invoke. So it is necessary but not sufficient.

Alt 2 — Move the gate to the SKILL instead of the workflow (skill is the durable enforcement surface, matching upstream's single <HARD-GATE>). The brainstorming skill already HAS the right shape (HARD-GATE + 9-item checklist + step-6 file write). The real defect is the wrapper diverging from it, so the cheapest durable fix is to make the wrapper stop competing, not to re-engineer the skill. Touching the skill here is over-reach for ISSUE 1 specifically (the skill is correct); reserve skill edits for the writing-plans menu issue.

Alt 3 — Delete discuss-phase.md's <process> entirely and let it be a one-line "run brainstorming for phase {N}" trigger. Cleanest against the CSO anti-pattern, but throws away the legitimate Quantis value-add (phase context load from ROADMAP, DECISIONS.md logging, the discuss/plan command split). Recommendation B keeps that value while subordinating it to the skill.

Alt 4 — Over-engineering to AVOID: do NOT add a self-refine loop, an adversarial reviewer, or a subagent here. SPEC.md non-production is an enforcement/contract bug, not a quality-depth bug; those belong to ISSUES 5/6.
rootCauseCategory: Primary: workflow/skill-layering defect (the thin wrapper paraphrases and competes with the skill it should merely trigger — the writing-skills CSO "follow the summary, skip the skill body" anti-pattern). Secondary/compounding: invented-mode ("discuss/explore mode"), missing-postcondition (no SPEC.md existence gate; completion banner is an unverified claim), and a broken artifact contract / path mismatch between producer (phase SPEC.md) and consumer (plan.md checks root SPEC.md). Positional-bias is a tertiary aggravator for where the gate must be placed (bookend, not buried).
tradeoffs: Adding a HARD-GATE + halt on step-9 makes /discuss-phase strictly stop before planning, which removes brainstorming's convenient auto-flow into writing-plans — users must run /plan as a second command. That is consistent with Quantis's existing <offer_next> design and the discuss/plan command split, so it is the intended behavior, but anyone relying on the upstream one-shot brainstorm→plan flow loses it inside /discuss-phase. The verifiable bash post-condition in the banner adds a small amount of workflow text and assumes the phase slug/number are resolvable at that point (plan.md already computes PHASE_SLUG/PHASE_DIR, so discuss-phase should reuse that exact derivation to stay consistent). Reconciling plan.md's lock to the phase path is a one-line change but must be coordinated so the root-level milestone SPEC.md "FINALIZED" check isn't silently dropped if it serves a separate milestone-gating purpose — confirm whether root SPEC.md "FINALIZED" is a milestone lock distinct from the phase spec before replacing it; if both matter, check both files rather than swapping one for the other.

diagnosisAssessment: All three of the user's claims are confirmed against the actual files. (a) "prefer SDD reads as ask-the-user": execute.md:72 literally says "When subagents are available, prefer .agents/skills/subagent-driven-development/SKILL.md". Per the research digest's writing-skills CSO finding, a wrapper that hedges/summarizes causes the model to follow the wrapper, not the skill — and "prefer" is exactly the soft language persuasion-principles flags as the OPPOSITE of a bright-line rule. Correct. (b) "writing-plans ALSO presents a menu (2nd entry point)": confirmed and WORSE than stated — it's in TWO places in writing-plans/SKILL.md: the Execution Handoff menu (lines 136-144: "Two execution options: 1. Subagent-Driven (recommended) 2. Inline Execution... Which approach?") AND the plan-header template (line 52: "REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use subagent-driven-development (recommended) or executing-plans"). The header template is then physically copied into every plan file (3.1-PLAN-A.md:9 and 3.1-PLAN-B.md:9 both contain "(recommended) or executing-plans"). Crucially, writing-plans' Execution Handoff IS the natural transition into execution (brainstorming step 9 -> writing-plans -> handoff), so the menu fires BEFORE /execute is ever typed. Correct, and a workflows-only fix cannot reach it. (c) "models confuse subagent with parallel and opt out for sequential work": confirmed, and the root is deeper than execute.md. SDD/SKILL.md's own When-to-Use diagram (lines 39-45) routes "Tasks mostly independent? -> no - tightly coupled -> Manual execution", and its description (line 4) says "with independent tasks". So the skill itself tells the model coupled work should NOT use SDD — yet SDD is sequential one-at-a-time (line 14, Red Flag line 253 "Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)"). The confusion is partly the skill's own contradictory routing, which a clarification note in execute.md cannot override.
whatsMissing: Four things the user/Phase-3.1 plan is not seeing. (1) The "prefer" word appears in execute.md in THREE places, not one: the callout at line 8 ("(or subagent-driven-development when subagents are available)"), line 72, and the <related> table at line 137 ("preferred when subagents available"). PLAN-B Task 1 only edits lines 66-78. Its own verification "grep -c prefer == 0" will FAIL because line 137's "preferred" and line 8's callout survive. The plan has a self-inconsistent acceptance test — it will report failure even after the intended edit. (2) executing-plans/SKILL.md:14 is a FOURTH hedge location ("When subagents are available... use subagent-driven-development instead"). (3) The bigger leak is the writing-plans Execution Handoff menu firing during the PLAN->EXECUTE transition, before /execute runs — so even a perfect execute.md leaves the menu as a live second entry point. The SPEC.md "Out of Scope: Skill files" boundary (line 120) directly contradicts the SPEC's own stated goal of "deterministic SDD"; the goal is unreachable inside the declared scope. (4) The subagent!=parallel confusion is seeded by SDD's own routing diagram and description ("independent tasks", "tightly coupled -> Manual execution"). A clarification NOTE in execute.md is necessary but not sufficient — the model that reads the skill body sees the contradictory flowchart. The note and the skill must agree.
recommendedFix: Resolve the scope decision: YES, edit the writing-plans skill. It is the single source-of-truth surface and the only place the second entry point can be removed. Concretely: (1) writing-plans/SKILL.md lines 134-152 (the whole "## Execution Handoff" menu) -> replace with the deterministic upstream rule: "After saving the plan, hand off to execution. On Antigravity, subagents are always available, so use subagent-driven-development. Announce: 'I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.' Do not present an execution-method menu." (2) writing-plans/SKILL.md line 52 header template -> change "Use subagent-driven-development (recommended) or executing-plans" to "REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: subagent-driven-development (Antigravity always has subagents). Use executing-plans only if subagent dispatch is unavailable." (3) executing-plans/SKILL.md:14 -> keep, it already points to SDD; just harden "When subagents are available" to "On Antigravity (subagents always available), use subagent-driven-development instead of this skill." (4) execute.md: fix ALL THREE locations (line 8 callout, line 72, line 137 table) plus apply PLAN-B's HARD-GATE block; and FIX PLAN-B's broken grep test — line 137 must change "preferred when subagents available" to "Execution methodology with two-stage review (SDD)" or the grep -c prefer==0 check fails. (5) The subagent!=parallel fix must touch SDD/SKILL.md itself, not just an execute.md note: amend the When-to-Use diagram so "tightly coupled" routes to SDD (sequential), and reword description line 4 from "with independent tasks" to "to execute a written implementation plan task-by-task in the current session" (per the CSO finding, the description must state WHEN, not summarize WHAT, and must not imply an independence precondition). The execute.md clarification note then reinforces, rather than fights, the skill.
alternativesConsidered: (A) Workflows-only (stay in declared SPEC scope): fix only execute.md. Rejected as insufficient — it cannot remove the writing-plans menu (the 2nd entry point that fires during PLAN->EXECUTE handoff) nor the per-plan-file "(recommended) or" header, and cannot fix SDD's contradictory routing diagram. This treats a symptom; the SPEC's own goal "deterministic SDD" is unreachable under "Out of Scope: skill files". (B) Delete executing-plans entirely / hard-code SDD everywhere: rejected — executing-plans is a legitimate non-subagent fallback (PLAN-B already keeps it for "subagent dispatch fails"), and deleting it diverges further from upstream, worsening upgradability. (C) Make SDD unconditional with NO fallback: rejected — the SDD Session Setup (lines 17-27) requires define_subagent/invoke_subagent; if dispatch fails you need a path forward, which PLAN-B's fallback line 67 correctly provides. (D) Leave a clarification note only for subagent!=parallel: rejected — the note is overridden by the skill's own routing flowchart the model reads next.
rootCauseCategory: Primary: instruction-bloat / hedged-language at a wrapper (the writing-skills CSO anti-pattern — "prefer"/"recommended"/"when available" soft qualifiers that make the model follow the summary and skip the bright-line rule). Secondary, and the load-bearing one the user under-weights: workflow/skill-layering — the authoritative methodology (the execution-method choice) lives in the writing-plans + executing-plans + SDD skills, so a workflows-only fix is structurally incapable of producing determinism (multiple-entry-point problem). Tertiary: a capability-description defect inside SDD itself (its When-to-Use diagram and "independent tasks" description actively mis-route coupled/sequential work away from SDD), which is the real source of the subagent!=parallel confusion.
tradeoffs: Editing forked superpowers skills is the user's stated upgradability concern, and it is real: writing-plans/SKILL.md is a near-verbatim v5.1.0 fork, so local edits create merge friction on the next upstream pull. Mitigations: (1) the edits ALIGN with upstream intent — upstream's own executing-plans says "if subagents available, use SDD"; Quantis is just resolving the menu to that rule because Antigravity ALWAYS has subagents, so the divergence is a specialization, not a fight. (2) Keep edits minimal and clearly marked (e.g. a "Quantis: Antigravity always has subagents" comment) so a 3-way merge is mechanical. (3) The alternative (never touching skills) imposes a PERMANENT correctness tax — a live second entry point and a contradictory routing diagram that no amount of workflow text can fully suppress; that is worse than a periodic merge. Risk of mandatory SDD: (a) overhead on trivial single-file plans (implementer + 2 reviewers per task = 3+ subagent invocations, real quota cost per the Antigravity quota-in-parallel warning) — acceptable because SDD is sequential, not parallel, so it does not multiply quota wildly, and the two-stage review is the quality floor the methodology exists to provide; (b) if subagent dispatch is genuinely unavailable, you MUST have the executing-plans fallback (PLAN-B:67 has it — keep it). There is no good reason to make SDD truly unconditional with no fallback. Net: "always SDD on Antigravity, executing-plans only as a dispatch-failure fallback" is correct; "SDD always, no exceptions ever" is over-reach.

diagnosisAssessment:
The user is RIGHT that the failure is real and that instructions are redundantly placed in execute.md and both execution skills. But the headline diagnosis ("tail of long files / positional bias") does not hold up against the actual files.

WHERE THE DIAGNOSIS IS WRONG (verified by line counts):
- execute.md is only 140 lines. The state-update instruction is Step 6 at lines 90-99 — roughly 2/3 down a SHORT file, and ROADMAP is first named at line 21. This is not a "lost in the middle" 50k-token document; lost-in-the-middle effects (Liu et al.) kick in on inputs approaching ~50% of the context window, not a 140-line markdown file. The model is not dropping Step 6 because it is buried; it drops it because by the time waves finish, execution has consumed huge context elsewhere (subagent dispatch, code, verify) and the controller never re-reads execute.md. The relevant phenomenon is conversation-history dilution + no enforced post-condition, NOT in-file positional bias.
- executing-plans.md (96 lines): ROADMAP update at line 91 — genuinely at the tail. SDD SKILL.md (300 lines): ROADMAP update at lines 296-300, the literal last 4 lines, under a "Quantis State Integration" appendix bolted onto a forked-upstream skill. So the "tail of long file" claim is ACCURATE for the SKILLS but FALSE for execute.md (the file the user named first).

WHERE THE DIAGNOSIS IS RIGHT:
- The instruction genuinely is duplicated in three places (execute.md:90-99, SDD:294-300, executing-plans:73-96) with no single authoritative owner — classic diffusion of responsibility; each location assumes another will do it.
- The "check ALL plans have SUMMARY.md, not ANY" catch from the user's own stress-test is correct and important.

A DEEPER BUG THE USER MISSED, found in the files: SUMMARY granularity is undefined and inconsistent. execute.md:75 says create `$PHASE-SUMMARY.md` (one file named after the PHASE) per plan — so two plans in phase 3.1 would BOTH try to write `3.1-SUMMARY.md` and collide/overwrite. Yet the discover step (execute.md:45) globs `*-SUMMARY.md` and pairs "PLAN without matching SUMMARY," implying one summary PER PLAN. Meanwhile real completed phases on disk show BOTH conventions: per-plan (v3.2/2.1-dynamic-skill-mcp/2.1-SUMMARY.md) AND per-task (v2.0/1/1-SUMMARY.md, 2-SUMMARY.md, 3-SUMMARY.md). The SDD/executing-plans skills never mention writing a SUMMARY at all — they only update STATE/JOURNAL/ROADMAP. So the pause.md safety net in PLAN-B keys off an artifact whose existence and naming is not guaranteed by the executor. The sync check is being built on sand.
whatsMissing:
1. The actual root cause is a MISSING ENFORCED POST-CONDITION, not positional bias. execute.md has no terminal gate that blocks "phase complete" until ROADMAP/STATE are verifiably updated. The instruction is prose ("Update State"), not a checkable action with a fail state. Per the research digest (Reflexion/Azure), models reliably perform verifiable tool-calls with post-conditions and silently skip prose reminders. The fix lever is "convert the must-do into a required action with a checkable post-condition," which the user's /pause safety net does NOT do at the source.

2. SUMMARY.md is an unreliable completion signal. The executor skills never write it; only execute.md:75 does, and with a colliding filename. Before any sync check can cross-reference SUMMARY files, the framework must (a) decide granularity (per-plan recommended, matching the discover-step pairing logic) and (b) make the executor reliably produce `{plan-name}-SUMMARY.md` not `$PHASE-SUMMARY.md`. Without this, the /pause check has both false negatives (no summary written -> phase looks incomplete forever) and false positives.

3. /pause is the WRONG and LATE boundary. /pause is for context-hygiene handoff and may be invoked mid-phase, on a break, after a debugging failure — not "phase done." Putting the canonical sync there means: a session that ends with /resume->keep-working or just hits the context limit (the very scenario pause.md:143-176 admits makes /pause unreachable) never gets synced. The reliable completion boundary is the END OF /execute and the PASS branch of /verify (verify.md:159-168 already rewrites STATE.md on PASS but does NOT touch ROADMAP — a real gap). The user is bolting the net onto the one workflow least guaranteed to run at phase completion.

4. /verify already half-implements the fix and is the better home. verify.md Step 5 "If PASS" updates STATE.md (lines 162-168) but conspicuously omits ROADMAP.md. /verify is the empirical "is this phase actually done" authority ("checks the CODEBASE, not SUMMARY claims," verify.md:24) — it should be the one place that marks ROADMAP ✅, gated on its own PASS verdict, using real codebase evidence rather than mere presence of a SUMMARY file.

5. The duplication itself is the bug. Three copies of the state-update instruction = three places to drift and zero owners. The research digest's CSO/diffusion warning applies: pick ONE authoritative owner.
recommendedFix:
Make ROADMAP/STATE sync a VERIFIED POST-CONDITION owned by /verify's PASS branch, and reduce /pause to a cheap read-only DRIFT DETECTOR — do not make /pause the corrector.

Concretely:

A) verify.md Step 5 "If PASS" (currently lines 162-168): add an explicit, gated ROADMAP update next to the existing STATE update. Phrase as a bright-line post-condition, not prose:
"<HARD-GATE> A phase is not VERIFIED until ROADMAP.md shows it ✅ Complete. Edit .quantis/ROADMAP.md to mark Phase {N} ✅ Complete now, then confirm with: grep 'Phase {N}.*✅' .quantis/ROADMAP.md . If the grep returns nothing, you have not finished. </HARD-GATE>"
This ties the ROADMAP write to verify's empirical PASS (codebase evidence) — the strongest, most trustworthy completion signal in the system — and gives it a checkable post-condition (the grep), which is exactly the enforcement pattern the research digest says works.

B) execute.md Step 6 (lines 90-99): keep it, but reframe its closing as a verifiable check identical to (A) — add the grep self-check after the commit so the executor confirms its own write landed. Add a one-line bright-line at the TOP of Step 5/6 ("STOP: phase is not complete until ROADMAP shows ✅ — verify with grep below") so it is bookended, not buried. This is the cheap primacy+recency fix for the genuinely tail-placed copies.

C) /pause Step 1.5 (PLAN-B): KEEP as a safety net but make it READ-ONLY DRIFT DETECTION that WARNS LOUDLY and does NOT silently auto-correct. /pause cannot trust SUMMARY presence as proof of completion (it has no codebase evidence — that is verify's job), so it must not mark ROADMAP ✅ on its own; doing so risks the "synthesize false progress" failure the Antigravity Auditor pattern warns against. It should print: "⚠️ Phase {N}: all plans have SUMMARY.md but ROADMAP is not ✅. Run /verify {N} to confirm and sync." This routes correction back to the authority instead of laundering an unverified completion.

D) Fix the SUMMARY-naming prerequisite FIRST (blocks PLAN-B). Change execute.md:75 from `$PHASE-SUMMARY.md` to a per-plan name (e.g. `{plan-basename}-SUMMARY.md`) so multiple plans don't collide, matching the discover-step pairing at line 45. Then the "ALL plans have a matching SUMMARY, not ANY" completeness condition becomes well-defined: for each *-PLAN.md there must be a corresponding *-SUMMARY.md.

E) The PLAN-B bash (lines 115-124) must implement "ALL not ANY": iterate PLAN files and require a matching SUMMARY for every one before considering the phase complete — the current loop fires on "any SUMMARY exists in dir," which is the exact false-positive the user's own stress-test flagged.
alternativesConsidered:
1. USER'S APPROACH (silent auto-correct in /pause): Simplest to ship, single file. But it treats the symptom, trusts an unreliable signal (SUMMARY presence) with no codebase evidence, sits on the least-reliable-to-run boundary, and risks false-completing partial phases. Rejected as the PRIMARY fix; acceptable only demoted to a read-only warning (option C).

2. SUBAGENT-ENFORCED POST-CONDITION (the user's last question): A dispatched "state-sync" subagent after the final task. Overkill and counter-indicated by the research digest — coordination/orchestration work should stay INLINE (controller never delegates its own bookkeeping); subagents are for isolated implementation/review, and a subagent has LESS context about what was done than the controller. The post-condition should be enforced inline via a grep self-check, not a subagent. Rejected.

3. A HOOK / deterministic script (the "guaranteed post-execution cleanup" pattern from other frameworks, e.g. git post-commit, CI required-checks): The truly robust answer is a non-LLM check — a hook that fails if a phase dir has all SUMMARYs but ROADMAP isn't ✅. Antigravity's model is skills + knowledge, not enforced hooks, and the user's settings.json hook mechanism could host this. Best long-term, but heavier; note it as the P2 follow-up, not the immediate fix.

4. MOVE the instruction in-file to the tail-end as recency (pure positional fix the user implied): Insufficient by itself — the controller doesn't re-read execute.md at end-of-waves, so where the line sits in the file is largely irrelevant. Only meaningful for the skills (executing-plans:91, SDD:296) and even there, a checkable post-condition beats repositioning.
rootCauseCategory: missing-postcondition (primary): no enforced, checkable terminal gate ties "phase complete" to a verified ROADMAP/STATE write. Secondary: instruction-duplication/diffusion-of-ownership (three copies in execute.md + SDD + executing-plans, no single owner) and an undefined-artifact-contract bug (SUMMARY.md granularity/naming inconsistent — `$PHASE-SUMMARY.md` collides; skills never write it). The user's "positional-bias/lost-in-the-middle" label is only marginally applicable (true for the two short skill tails, false for the 140-line execute.md).
tradeoffs:
- Moving the canonical sync to /verify's PASS branch means a user who skips /verify never gets the authoritative ROADMAP update. Mitigated by keeping execute.md Step 6 as a self-checked write AND the /pause read-only warning as the backstop — three layers, but only ONE (verify) is authoritative, avoiding the diffusion problem because the others either self-verify (execute) or merely warn-and-route (pause).
- Read-only /pause warning (vs silent auto-fix) is slightly more friction for the user (they must run /verify) but eliminates the false-positive risk of marking an unverified phase complete. This is the right trade for a methodology whose core principle (verify.md:24) is 'no trust me, it works.'
- Fixing SUMMARY naming touches execute.md:75 and may need a note that legacy phases used per-task summaries; low risk since it is forward-looking.
- The grep post-condition adds one bash call per completion but is the cheapest reliable enforcement; aligns with the digest's 'verifiable action beats prose' finding.

diagnosisAssessment:
The user's core diagnosis is CORRECT but understated. They are right that (a) the verification-before-completion skill ref lives in a metadata block, not a numbered process step (verify.md line 35: `**Skill reference:** ...` and line 37 blockquote `> Before starting verification, read and follow...`), and (b) /plan and /execute put their skill handoff inside numbered steps (plan.md line 135 `## 4. Delegate to Skill` -> "Read and follow ... writing-plans/SKILL.md exactly"; execute.md line 71 `## 4. Execute Waves` step 2 -> "Read and follow ... executing-plans/SKILL.md"). That asymmetry is real.

But they MISSED a more severe, mechanical bug that strengthens their case: verify.md's `<context>` tag opens at line 27 and is NEVER CLOSED (confirmed: `grep -c "</context>" verify.md` = 0; it is the ONLY workflow of 30 with mismatched context tags). Tag order is `<context>`(27) -> `<process>`(39) -> `</process>`(248) -> `<evidence_requirements>` -> `<related>`(closes 289), with no `</context>` anywhere. So structurally the ENTIRE process section (all 6 steps, evidence requirements, related) is nested inside the open `<context>` metadata block. The skill ref is not merely "near" metadata — the whole workflow body is syntactically inside the metadata wrapper. This makes "models treat <context> as background" bite far harder than the user argued.

Two refinements to their framing: (1) verify.md DOES already contain an imperative "read and follow" (line 37), just phrased as a blockquote and mis-located — so this is a positioning/strength problem, not a total absence. (2) The deeper reason it gets skipped is NOT only position: the skill's own description (SKILL.md line 3, "Use when about to claim work is complete...") is the progressive-disclosure auto-trigger, and using-quantis line 114 only maps /verify->verification-before-completion in a table. Nothing FORCES the load; it relies on the model noticing it should self-trigger.
whatsMissing:
1) THE UNCLOSED TAG. verify.md line 27 `<context>` has no matching `</context>`. This is a concrete parse-level defect unique to verify.md among all 30 workflows, and it subsumes the user's "buried in metadata" concern — fixing it is a prerequisite for any Step 1.5 to land in a real `<process>` scope.

2) THE AUDIT ANSWER (all 30 workflows checked): Exactly ONE other workflow has the identical metadata-vs-step pattern: debug.md. Its `systematic-debugging` skill ref sits in a properly-CLOSED `<context>` block (lines 16-22, ref at line 19, blockquote at line 21 "Before starting, read and follow the systematic-debugging skill") with NO re-invocation inside its 8 numbered steps. verify.md and debug.md are byte-for-byte the same anti-pattern (the only difference: debug.md closes its tag). A fix to verify.md that ignores debug.md leaves the bug half-fixed.

A weaker third instance: new-project.md line 132 references brainstorming as a `>` blockquote "Skill enhancement: For structured ideation, read..." inside <process> but NOT as a numbered mandatory step — softer/optional framing, lower severity.

For contrast, the workflows that get it RIGHT (skill ref inside a numbered ## step within <process>): discuss-phase.md (## 2, line 29), research-phase.md (## 2, line 33), stress-test.md (## 2, line 29), map.md (## 2, line 20), update-plan.md (## 3, line 42), plan.md (## 4, line 135), execute.md (## 4 step 2, line 71). (Several of these carry the separate invented-mode bug — ISSUE 1/2 — but their skill POSITIONING is correct.)

3) "Read and follow X" is NOT strong enough on its own per the research: it is hedged prose with no post-condition. The lost-in-the-middle evidence (Liu et al., 20-30pt mid-context drop) plus the Iron Law pattern in the skill itself (SKILL.md lines 17-38: "NO COMPLETION CLAIMS WITHOUT FRESH VERIFICATION EVIDENCE" + numbered Gate Function "Skip any step = lying") show the durable enforcement surface is the SKILL, not the wrapper. The wrapper should TRIGGER + bookend, not paraphrase.

4) The user is missing that verify.md never re-invokes the skill anywhere in its numbered steps — there is no fallback. /plan and /execute embed the handoff mid-process; verify.md has it only in the (broken) header.
recommendedFix:
Three coordinated edits to verify.md, plus one to debug.md.

verify.md:
(A) CLOSE THE TAG. Insert `</context>` immediately before line 39 `<process>`. This is the load-bearing fix — without it, any Step 1.5 still lives inside an open metadata block.

(B) ADD NUMBERED STEP 1.5 (or renumber: make it the new Step 1, before "Load Verification Context"), phrased as a required action with a verifiable post-condition and Authority framing, NOT hedged prose. Concretely:
"## 1.5 Load Verification Methodology
**STOP. Read and follow `.agents/skills/verification-before-completion/SKILL.md` now.** Announce: 'Using verification-before-completion to validate Phase {N}.' This skill's Iron Law (no completion claims without fresh evidence) and Gate Function govern every PASS/FAIL verdict below. Do NOT record any must-have status until you have loaded it."
Place it near the TOP (primacy) so it is not in the low-attention middle.

(C) BOOKEND (recency). The workflow already has an <evidence_requirements> section near the end (lines 250-272); add a closing post-condition there: "Before writing any PASS verdict in VERIFICATION.md, confirm you applied the verification-before-completion Gate Function (identify command -> run fresh -> read output -> verify). If you did not load the skill, halt and load it." This satisfies the Microsoft/Azure "state at top, repeat at end, structured headings" recipe and the brainstorming HARD-GATE shape the framework already uses.

debug.md: apply the same Step 1.5 promotion — move the line 19/21 skill ref out of <context> into a numbered "## 1. Load Debugging Methodology" step with the same STOP + announce phrasing. (debug.md's tag is already closed, so no tag fix needed.)

Optionally upgrade new-project.md line 132 from a `>` "enhancement" blockquote to a numbered step if brainstorming is meant to be mandatory there.
alternativesConsidered:
ALT 1 — Just add Step 1.5, leave the unclosed tag (the user's literal proposal). Rejected: the new step would still be nested inside the open `<context>` block, so it inherits the exact "this is background" treatment the user is trying to escape. The tag fix is non-optional.

ALT 2 — Rely on the skill's description auto-trigger (progressive disclosure) and using-quantis line 114, do nothing to verify.md. Rejected: this is precisely the unreliable mechanism that produced the bug; the research shows description-based triggering is best-effort, and the framework's own pattern is to force the load at the workflow entry point.

ALT 3 — Inline the whole Gate Function into verify.md instead of referencing the skill. Rejected as anti-pattern: the writing-skills CSO finding is that when a wrapper summarizes a skill's process, the model follows the summary and skips the skill body (the proven two-stage-review bug). The wrapper must TRIGGER, not paraphrase. Keep the methodology in the skill (the durable surface), reference + bookend from the workflow.

ALT 4 — A full structural HARD-GATE tag (like brainstorming's `<HARD-GATE>`) wrapping the verdict step. Reasonable and slightly stronger than (C); I folded its spirit into the bookended post-condition. A literal `<HARD-GATE>` is fine too and matches upstream phrasing, but the bookend is lower-risk to retrofit into the existing <evidence_requirements> section.

ALT 5 — Convert the skill load into a required tool-call artifact (e.g., the model must emit "skill loaded" confirmation). Strongest per the Reflexion/post-condition evidence; (B)'s "Announce: 'Using ...'" is the lightweight version of this and is what using-superpowers/using-quantis already mandate.
rootCauseCategory: Primarily positional-bias / missing-postcondition (skill handoff placed in a low-attention metadata block instead of a numbered process step with a verifiable gate), compounded by a concrete markup defect (unclosed `<context>` tag) that nests the entire process body inside metadata. Systemic category: workflow/skill-layering — the thin workflow fails to reliably TRIGGER the skill at its entry point. NOT instruction-bloat and NOT invented-modes (those are ISSUES 1/2, which are orthogonal and afflict different workflows).
tradeoffs:
- Closing the tag + adding Step 1.5 is near-zero-risk and small (a few lines). The only cost is mild redundancy between the new header step and the closing bookend, which is intentional (primacy + recency) and aligns with the framework's own HARD-GATE pattern.
- Promoting debug.md adds a second file to the change set, slightly widening Phase 3.1 scope, but it is the same edit and leaving it creates an inconsistent codebase where two sibling workflows that share a pattern get fixed differently. Cheap to do now; expensive to rediscover later.
- "STOP/announce/halt" Authority phrasing risks sounding heavy in a docs-style file; this is the deliberate persuasion tradeoff (Authority + Commitment > hedged prose) the framework's own research endorses (Meincke 2025). Avoid going further into Liking/Reciprocity.
- Renumbering to make verification the new Step 1 (vs inserting "1.5") is cleaner long-term but touches more line numbers; "1.5" minimizes diff. Either is acceptable.
- A bookended gate does NOT guarantee the model loads the skill — no prompt-level fix is a hard runtime guarantee. It maximizes probability via primacy+recency+post-condition, which is the best available lever short of a tool-enforced hook.

diagnosisAssessment:
CORRECT on the gap's existence and shape, WRONG on one premise. What the user got right: there is no whole-phase comprehensive code review anywhere in the actual flow. Verified against files: (1) /verify (verify.md:8-19, 39-156) is purely the empirical-evidence Auditor — it extracts must-haves, runs commands/screenshots, writes VERIFICATION.md. It has ZERO code-quality pass — no file-by-file, bug, dead-code, or edge-case review. Its <related> table (verify.md:284-288) lists only verification-before-completion and context-health-monitor, no code review. (2) /execute (execute.md:66-99) step 4 delegates execution to executing-plans/SDD, step 5 (lines 79-84) only re-checks must-haves against the codebase, step 6 commits — no senior review node. (3) requesting-code-review is scoped per-task: SKILL.md:14-17 mandatory triggers are 'after each task in SDD', 'after completing major feature', 'before merge to main'; the template (code-reviewer.md) reviews a single {BASE_SHA}..{HEAD_SHA} task range. So the user is right that per-task review ≠ whole-phase review.

Where the user is WRONG: the claim 'SDD does ... one final reviewer' implies a final whole-implementation reviewer already runs and just isn't phase-level. It exists only as a DANGLING GRAPH NODE, not a wired, reliably-firing capability. subagent-driven-development/SKILL.md:77 and :96-97 show 'Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation' -> 'Use finishing-a-development-branch skill' — but (a) it lives ONLY inside the SDD skill body, (b) /execute never references it (execute.md step 5 jumps straight to must-have checks), (c) it only fires IF SDD was even chosen, and execute.md:8 and :137 hedge SDD as merely 'preferred when subagents available' (the ISSUE 4 defect), so on the inline executing-plans path NO final reviewer runs at all, and (d) it has no batching/overflow handling — it just reuses code-reviewer.md over the whole diff, which overflows on a multi-plan phase. So the user does this manually today precisely because the 'final reviewer' is an unreachable/unreliable node, not because the concept is missing. That nuance matters for the fix: this is mostly a WIRING + ROBUSTNESS problem layered on a real capability gap, not a greenfield build.
whatsMissing:
1) The existing code-reviewer.md is ALREADY titled 'Senior Code Reviewer' (code-reviewer.md:12) with the exact Critical/Important/Minor + 'Ready to merge? [Yes|No|With fixes]' rubric (lines 83-103) the research recommends. The user is about to design from scratch a template that already exists — reuse it, don't reinvent.

2) That template is a SINGLE MONOLITHIC prompt checking 5 dimensions at once — Plan alignment, Code quality, Architecture, Testing, Production readiness (code-reviewer.md:35-63). Research (Branch-Solve-Merge, +26% human agreement; LLM-judge ceiling 3-5 dims/call) says one monolithic 5-dim prompt is ABOVE the evidence-based ceiling and produces SHALLOW review — which is likely WHY the user finds it inadequate and reviews manually. A 'new skill' that copies this monolithic shape will reproduce the shallowness.

3) The template has NONE of the false-positive controls that are the actual quality lever: no 'What NOT to Flag' negative list, no 0-100 confidence scoring/threshold, no tier-gate (a 5-line phase would spawn the full review), no diff-batching/overflow ladder. code-reviewer.md:28-31 inlines the FULL 'git diff {BASE}..{HEAD}' into the prompt — this is exactly the overflow the user fears, and it's unmanaged.

4) Context-isolation point the user missed: per Antigravity, subagents return only a distilled summary and the orchestrator never sees full work. A whole-phase reviewer must read the cumulative phase diff itself (read-only 'research' subagent), NOT trust the SUMMARY.md files — those are the implementers' self-reports (the 'synthesize false progress' risk).

5) The before/after-/verify question has a clean answer the user hasn't resolved: code review and verification check DIFFERENT things (review = is the code good; verify = does it meet must-haves with evidence). Sequencing matters because fixing review findings can change behavior that verify must then re-confirm.
recommendedFix:
Build a thin senior-code-review CAPABILITY as a PHASE-LEVEL gate, reusing existing assets, with map-reduce fan-out — NOT a from-scratch monolithic skill.

WHERE: A new step in /execute (execute.md), inserted as Step 5 BEFORE the existing must-have check (renumber current 5->6, 6->7), gated to run once after the LAST plan's tasks in the phase complete. Rationale for BEFORE /verify: review fixes can alter behavior, so review-then-verify means /verify confirms the FINAL code; running /verify first then review would invalidate the verification when review fixes land. This also matches the upstream sequence (SDD: final reviewer -> finishing-a-development-branch) and Cloudflare's review-before-merge gate.

WHAT (concrete):
1. RISK-TIER GATE FIRST (Cloudflare template). Compute fileCount + changed lines over the phase diff (BASE = phase-start SHA, HEAD = last commit): trivial (<=10 lines, <=2 files) -> skip with a logged note; small -> 2 aspect lanes; large/security-or-migration-touching -> full aspect fan-out. Prevents swarming a 5-line phase.
2. MAP = dispatch N read-only 'research' subagents (invoke_subagent) fanning out BY ASPECT, one criterion each (BSM decomposition), NOT one generic reviewer per file: correctness/bugs, dead-code & simplification, edge-cases, tests, convention/CONSTITUTION.md compliance. Each gets the per-file diff (write patches to a temp dir, pass the PATH, not inline diff) plus view_file/grep tool access to pull cross-file context on demand. Each returns a 1-2k-token findings summary only.
3. Each lane prompt carries an explicit 'What NOT to Flag' list (unchanged code the phase didn't touch, defense-in-depth when primary defense is adequate, library-swap suggestions, lint/style/naming nits) and requires every finding to carry a 0-100 confidence + file:line.
4. REDUCE = the /execute orchestrator (or one synthesizer subagent) dedupes (a finding from two lanes kept once), re-categorizes, re-reads cited source to kill speculative findings, drops confidence <80 in default mode, and emits ONE report ranked Critical/Important/Minor with a Ready-to-merge verdict. Write PHASE-REVIEW.md into $PHASE_DIR.
5. REUSE the existing 'Senior Code Reviewer' rubric and verdict format from code-reviewer.md verbatim for the synthesizer's output — do not invent new severity language.
6. Make it a real gate with Authority phrasing per persuasion-principles: 'YOU MUST run phase review before marking the phase complete' + a TodoWrite item, NOT 'optionally consider'. Critical findings block phase-complete; Important must be fixed or explicitly deferred to TODO.md.

Package the reusable parts (tier function, aspect lane prompts, 'What NOT to Flag' list, synthesizer prompt) as files under a new .agents/skills/senior-code-review/ dir (mirroring how SDD ships separate *-prompt.md files), and have /execute TRIGGER + ANNOUNCE the skill rather than paraphrase it (avoids the CSO wrapper-summary anti-pattern). Also fix the dangling SDD node: change SKILL.md:96-97 to point the 'final code reviewer' at this same senior-code-review skill so there is ONE implementation, two entry points (per-task review stays in requesting-code-review; whole-phase review is this new skill).
alternativesConsidered:
A) Integrate as an OPTIONAL step in /verify (the user's alternative). Rejected as primary: /verify is the empirical-evidence Auditor (verify.md:8-19); bolting code review onto it conflates two distinct concerns (is-the-code-good vs does-it-meet-must-haves) and, being 'optional', would be skipped under lost-in-the-middle — the very ISSUE-3-class defect. Also wrong order: review belongs before verify so verify confirms post-fix code.

B) Single monolithic whole-phase reviewer (just call existing code-reviewer.md over the full phase diff). Rejected: this is essentially the dangling SDD final-reviewer node that already exists and that the user already finds inadequate. It overflows on multi-plan phases (inlined full diff, code-reviewer.md:28-31) and is shallow (5 dims in one prompt, above the 3-5 ceiling).

C) Brand-new from-scratch skill with its own reviewer template. Rejected as wasteful/risky: code-reviewer.md already is a Senior reviewer with the right rubric; a new template invites divergence and the same monolithic shape.

D) Make it phase-level inside SDD only (don't touch /execute). Rejected: SDD isn't guaranteed to run (execute.md hedges SDD), and the inline executing-plans path would then get NO whole-phase review. The gate must live in /execute so it fires regardless of execution method.

E) Parallel implementers-style concern: note that fanning out REVIEW aspects in parallel does NOT violate the 'SDD never runs parallel implementers' rule — reviewers are read-only and independent, so parallel aspect-fan-out is safe and is the recommended map shape.
rootCauseCategory: capability-gap (primary) compounded by workflow-layering/wiring failure and positional-bias. The whole-phase senior review capability is genuinely absent from the runnable flow, BUT the building blocks exist and were left unwired: the SDD 'final code reviewer' is a dangling graph node never referenced by /execute, and the executing-plans path bypasses it entirely. It is NOT invented-modes or instruction-bloat. Secondary: the existing reviewer template is a monolithic >ceiling prompt (a quality/design defect, not a missing piece).
tradeoffs: COST/QUOTA: Antigravity bills parallel subagents against the same quota concurrently; an aspect fan-out of 4-6 read-only reviewers per phase is real spend. The tier-gate (skip trivial, 2 lanes for small) is the mitigation and must not be optional. SEQUENCING vs SPEED: review-before-verify means a re-run of /verify after review fixes — slightly slower but correct; the alternative (verify-first) risks shipping unverified post-fix code. FALSE-NEGATIVE risk of confidence>=80 default: aggressive thresholds suppress real-but-uncertain findings; mitigate by mapping to the harness's existing /code-review effort levels (low/medium = high-confidence only; high/max = surface uncertain), so the user can dial depth. SCOPE CREEP into Phase 3.1: ISSUE 5 is NOT in the current 3.1 SPEC (confirmed: SPEC.md has no senior/whole-phase/code-review content) and depends on the pending ISSUE-4 fix (deterministic SDD) to make the SDD final-reviewer node reliably reachable; building this before resolving the writing-plans/execute determinism decision means wiring a gate onto a path that may still hedge SDD. MAINTENANCE: adds a new skill dir + a step to /execute; acceptable since it reuses code-reviewer.md and the existing subagent primitives. Acceptance bar to stress-test against: >50% addressed-finding rate, <10% (ideally <5%) false-positive rate, linter/style nits excluded.

diagnosisAssessment:
Verified against the actual files. CORRECT parts: (1) The invented-mode bug is real and worse than Issue 1 — it appears THREE times in stress-test.md, not once: the H1 title (line 6) "# /stress-test → brainstorming skill (critique mode)", the subtitle (line 8) "Critique methodology is powered by `brainstorming`", and step 2 (line 29) "Read `.agents/skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md` and adopt adversarial critique mode." Confirmed brainstorming/SKILL.md has zero notion of modes: it is a 9-step linear checklist (lines 20-33) whose terminal state auto-invokes writing-plans (line 66), and its Visual Companion is explicitly "Available as a tool — not a mode" (line 149). So pointing stress-test AT brainstorming is not just an invented qualifier — it is a category error: brainstorming is a constructive ideation→SPEC→writing-plans pipeline, the polar opposite of a destructive falsification pass. If the model actually followed the skill it was told to "adopt," it would start asking clarifying questions and try to write a SPEC, not attack one. The delegation is inert at best, actively wrong at worst. (2) The "7 dimensions in ONE pass / ONE context" observation is correct (lines 33-71, single agent, single window) and the user's instinct that this is why it stays shallow is well-founded.

WHERE THE DIAGNOSIS IS INCOMPLETE: The user frames this as "model OR prompt OR design" and fixates on the mode bug + fan-out. The single most damaging defect is one the user did not name: the report template REQUIRES fix suggestions. Line 80: "**Suggestion:** {how to fix}" and step 5 (line 85) "Update Spec." The adversarial-review research (obra/superpowers #1130) is explicit that the moment a reviewer proposes improvements "it drifts back into the same mode the existing skills already cover and the findings get softer." A mandatory Suggestion field per finding is a built-in softening mechanism — it converts an adversary into a helpful editor. This, plus the absence of any "produce a concrete failing scenario per finding" requirement, is the real shallowness driver, more than the dimension count. There is also no fresh-context guarantee anywhere in the file: nothing stops the same agent that wrote the SPEC from running /stress-test on its own work, and same-author self-review has documented systematic blind spots.
whatsMissing:
1. The Suggestion field IS a depth bug. The user attributes shallowness to "one pass / 7 dimensions," but line 80's mandatory `**Suggestion:**` is independently causing soft findings — it pulls the model out of falsification into fix-it mode on every finding. Fan-out alone will not fix this; you would just get 7 parallel agents each emitting soft suggestion-laden findings.

2. The bug is triple, not single. Removing only line 29 leaves the title (line 6) and subtitle (line 8) still asserting "critique mode" / "powered by brainstorming." Per the writing-skills CSO finding, the model reads the wrapper's framing; a top-of-file "(critique mode)" anchored by primacy is exactly the high-attention zone that overrides the skill body.

3. Delegating to brainstorming at all is the root error — not the word "mode." Even with a clean qualifier, brainstorming is the wrong skill (constructive, ends in writing-plans). Stress-test has NO correct skill to delegate to today: there is no adversarial-review skill or prompt template in the repo (confirmed: only spec-document-reviewer-prompt.md, plan-document-reviewer-prompt.md, code-reviewer.md, spec-reviewer-prompt.md exist — all constructive/approval-biased). So the "skill-powered workflow" header is a fiction; stress-test's 7-dimension framework (lines 33-71) is actually self-contained and owes nothing to brainstorming.

4. Fan-out has real cost the user underweights. The research digest's Antigravity findings are explicit: subagents bill quota in parallel ("exhaust weekly quota in a couple tasks" on the $200 tier) and the SDK pauses the parent per ToolResult, so 7 dimension-agents largely serialize anyway. Fan-out is the right SHAPE (BSM: +26% human-agreement, evidence ceiling 3-5 dims/judge call) but 7 separate subagents for a ~110-line SPEC review is over-engineered relative to the depth win.

5. Stress-test currently has no risk-tier gate. It runs the full 7-dimension machine on a 5-line config-change SPEC the same as a 50-file platform SPEC. The senior-review research's #1 lever is tier-gating FIRST.

6. Missing falsification scaffolding: no "What NOT to Flag" list, no per-finding confidence, no required failing scenario, no fixed attack angles (Structural / Operational-"how it breaks at 3am" / Scope-creep). These prompt-level mechanisms are what create depth — they are cheaper and higher-leverage than parallelism.

7. ISSUE 6 is genuinely unscoped (good): Phase 3.1 SPEC (lines 111-122) explicitly limits scope to discuss-phase/execute/verify/pause and lists "Out of Scope: Skill files... New features." A new adversarial-review skill/prompt is net-new design, not a 3.1 reliability fix — do not bolt it onto 3.1.
recommendedFix:
Replace the brainstorming delegation with a dedicated adversarial-review prompt template, restructured around FALSIFICATION, and reframe the 7 dimensions as 3 fixed attack ANGLES. Concretely, three edits, all idiomatic to the repo's existing dedicated-prompt-file convention:

(A) Create `.agents/skills/requesting-code-review/adversarial-reviewer-prompt.md` (sibling to the existing code-reviewer.md / spec-reviewer-prompt.md — this is the established pattern: SDD ships spec-reviewer-prompt.md + code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md as separate files). Model it on obra/superpowers #1130:
   - Persona: "You are an adversarial reviewer. Your job is to BREAK this spec, not improve it. Assume it is wrong and prove it."
   - DIAGNOSIS ONLY — explicitly: "Do NOT propose fixes. The moment you suggest an improvement, you stop being an adversary. Report the flaw and how it breaks. Nothing else." (This deletes the line-80 `**Suggestion:**` field — the single highest-leverage change.)
   - Three fixed attack angles (collapse the 7 dimensions into these; 7→3 respects the 3-5 dims/judge evidence ceiling): STRUCTURAL (hidden coupling, load-bearing assumptions nobody wrote down, decisions that look reversible but aren't — absorbs Consistency/Feasibility/Maintainability), OPERATIONAL ("how does it break at 3am; recovery when step 4 commits and step 5 dies; missing rollback" — absorbs Edge Cases/Security/Performance), SCOPE ("where does 'simple' quietly grow; the second system hiding inside the first" — absorbs Completeness/YAGNI).
   - HARD requirement per finding: (1) a concrete failing scenario ("walk through the exact sequence where this breaks"), (2) severity + impact tag, (3) a 0-100 confidence; instruct the synthesizer to drop anything below ~80 in default mode.
   - A "What NOT to Flag" list: style/wording nits, defense-in-depth when primary defense is adequate, improvements (those belong to brainstorming, not here).

(B) Rewrite stress-test.md to TRIGGER + ANNOUNCE that prompt instead of "adopting brainstorming mode." Delete the three mode references: line 6 title → "# /stress-test — adversarial spec review", delete the line-8 "powered by brainstorming" subtitle, line 29 → "Read and follow `.agents/skills/requesting-code-review/adversarial-reviewer-prompt.md`." Add a top-anchored HARD-GATE: "This is a falsification pass. Diagnosis only — no fix suggestions. Each finding MUST include a concrete failing scenario." Restate it as a closing post-condition (bookend, per lost-in-the-middle). Remove the line-80 Suggestion field from the report template; keep Severity (lines 83) but add the confidence threshold.

(C) Add a fresh-context requirement: dispatch ONE adversarial-reviewer subagent (Antigravity research/define_subagent type) that did NOT author the SPEC, in a clean window, reading only the SPEC + ROADMAP + DECISIONS. This is the load-bearing independence property — more impactful than parallel fan-out.

Do NOT fan out 7 dimension-subagents as the primary fix. One fresh-context adversary running the 3-angle falsification prompt will be dramatically deeper than today's in-context brainstorming-mode pass, at 1 subagent of cost. Reserve per-angle fan-out (3 agents, not 7) as an optional "deep" tier gated by SPEC size.
alternativesConsidered:
ALT 1 — User's literal proposal: one subagent per dimension (7 agents), independent model, one pass. Tradeoff: BSM evidence supports decomposition (+26% agreement) and independence (3-5x more issues), so the SHAPE is right, but 7 lanes exceeds the 3-5 dims/judge ceiling, burns parallel quota (Antigravity bills concurrently), mostly serializes anyway (SDK pauses parent per ToolResult), and — critically — does nothing about the line-80 Suggestion-field softening. You'd get 7 parallel shallow-and-soft reviews. Rejected as primary; folded in as an optional deep tier at 3 lanes (one per attack angle).

ALT 2 — Minimal Phase-3.1-style patch: just delete "in ... mode" at line 29 and stop. Tradeoff: cheapest, consistent with Issues 1-4 treatment. Rejected: it treats the symptom. Even mode-free, you're delegating an adversarial task to a constructive skill (brainstorming ends in writing-plans), and the Suggestion field still softens every finding. This is the "treats a symptom" trap the task warned about.

ALT 3 — Build a full senior-code-review-style map-reduce capability for stress-test (tier-gate → N aspect-agents → coordinator dedupe/re-categorize/verify/rank). Tradeoff: this is the gold standard from the senior-review research and is the right model for ISSUE 5 (code review). Rejected for ISSUE 6: spec/plan review is text-only and far smaller than a code changeset; the coordinator/dedupe/diff-chunking machinery is overkill for a ~110-line SPEC. The single-adversary + optional-3-lane design captures ~80% of the depth at ~15% of the complexity.

ALT 4 — Keep stress-test inline (no subagent) but just harden the prompt (diagnosis-only, attack angles, failing-scenario, confidence). Tradeoff: zero quota cost, captures most of the depth win from the prompt restructure. Weakness: no fresh-context independence — the same session that wrote the SPEC reviews it, with documented self-review blind spots. Reasonable as a v1 if subagent dispatch is friction; recommend pairing prompt-hardening (must-have) with fresh-context dispatch (high-value).
rootCauseCategory: Primary: wrong-skill-delegation + invented-modes (stress-test points an adversarial task at the constructive brainstorming skill via a 3x-repeated "critique mode" qualifier the skill doesn't define). Secondary and equally load-bearing: missing-falsification-discipline (mandatory `**Suggestion:**` fix field at line 80 + step-5 "Update Spec" softens every finding into a helpful edit; no diagnosis-only constraint, no required failing scenario, no fixed attack angles, no confidence threshold, no "what not to flag", no fresh-context independence). Tertiary: capability-gap (the repo has no adversarial-review skill or prompt template — the "skill-powered workflow" header is fiction; the 7-dimension framework is actually self-contained). Positional-bias is a minor contributor (the "(critique mode)" anchor sits in the high-attention title, reinforcing the wrong frame).
tradeoffs: The recommended fix adds one net-new file (adversarial-reviewer-prompt.md) and a subagent dispatch, so it is heavier than the trivial line-29 deletion and crosses the Phase 3.1 "no new skill files / no new features" scope boundary — it must be scoped as its own phase, not smuggled into 3.1. Collapsing 7 named dimensions into 3 attack angles risks losing the discoverability of the explicit Security/Performance labels users may rely on; mitigate by listing the old dimensions as example probes UNDER each angle so nothing is lost. Diagnosis-only (no Suggestion field) trades user convenience (they no longer get a fix handed to them) for finding depth — but that is the entire point of an adversarial pass, and brainstorming/plan already own the constructive side, so the fix lives elsewhere in the pipeline, not nowhere. Fresh-context dispatch costs quota and a parent-pause; for small SPECs the inline-hardened-prompt variant (ALT 4) is an acceptable cheaper fallback. The confidence-threshold drop (<80) risks hiding real-but-uncertain findings; expose it as a "thorough" mode toggle mapped to effort level, mirroring the harness's existing low/medium/high/max review levels.
